candidates.item: Technology-agnostic: no vendor lock-in, no prescribed frameworks. Engineering stack spans Solidity, Rust, Vyper; EVM-compatible and non-EVM chains (Solana, Sui, Aptos); L1/L2; on-prem or cloud-native.
candidates.why_interesting: Most boutique Web3 shops plant a flag on one chain or one VM (e.g., 'we're an EVM house'). Genuinely spanning EVM + Solana + Sui + Aptos + L1/L2 simultaneously is a real engineering commitment, not just a checkbox — it means separate Rust vs. Solidity competencies co-existing in one team. The 'no prescribed frameworks' stance is also a pointed contrast to the wave of cookie-cutter scaffold shops. HOWEVER: 'technology-agnostic / no lock-in' is a very common marketing claim in consultancies. The specific chain breadth (Sui, Aptos alongside Solana and EVM) is the genuinely differentiating detail here.
candidates.recency_note: Sourced from company website/GitHub; site is still loading as a JS app so content may not yet be publicly rendered.

candidates.item: Full IP ownership for clients. No licensing fees, no ongoing royalties.
candidates.why_interesting: Many dev shops retain some IP or charge ongoing support/licensing as a revenue model. A clean, explicit 'zero royalties, zero licensing fees, full IP transfer' is a bold commercial stance — especially in smart contract work where the code literally holds money. It's a trust signal aimed squarely at enterprise buyers who've been burned by vendor lock-in. Still, this is worth verifying whether it's truly universal or only applies to certain engagement types.
candidates.recency_note: Sourced from company-provided research notes; homepage content not yet publicly rendered.

candidates.item: GitHub orgs include forks of wagmi, EIPs, Superfluid Protocol Monorepo, prysm, and protocol-studies — alongside a custom SaaS Boilerplate (Next.js + Tailwind CSS + Shadcn UI + TypeScript).
candidates.why_interesting: Forking prysm (Ethereum consensus client, written in Go) and protocol-studies alongside application-layer tools like wagmi and Superfluid signals genuine protocol-depth engineering, not just dApp scaffolding. Most boutique Web3 consultancies live entirely at the application layer. The juxtaposition of consensus-layer forks with a polished SaaS frontend boilerplate suggests a genuinely full-stack, protocol-to-product range — a verifiable, concrete detail rather than a marketing claim.
candidates.recency_note: GitHub org content is live and publicly inspectable as of research date.

candidates.item: Founded by seasoned crypto investors and blockchain engineers who understand both technical and economic layers of decentralized systems. Backed by crypto-native investors with capital markets expertise and ecosystem connections.
candidates.why_interesting: The dual founder profile — investors who can model tokenomics AND engineers who can ship smart contracts — is a genuine differentiator from pure-dev shops or pure-advisory firms. Most consultancies are one or the other. The 'capital markets expertise' framing also suggests they can engage at the tokenomics/treasury level, not just at the code level. Candidate for a strong opener if Jose is a founder. Cannot yet be confirmed for Jose specifically.
candidates.recency_note: Sourced from company-provided research notes; unverifiable from live site until homepage renders.

candidates.item: Two separate GitHub organisations exist: github.com/Block-Sherpa and github.com/Block-Sherpa-WorkPlace — both listing blockcsherpa.dev and hello@blockcsherpa.dev as contacts.
candidates.why_interesting: Operating two distinct GitHub orgs (one likely client-facing/open, one internal/workplace) at what appears to be a very early-stage company is a subtle but telling operational maturity signal — they've thought about separating public-facing repos from internal work product. Small but verifiable, and not something every early-stage shop bothers to set up.
candidates.recency_note: GitHub orgs are live and publicly visible as of research date.
